Title: TE David Senters v. Savannah River
Summary: TE David Senters filed a complaint challenging the examination of TE Jonathan Stamberg by the Savannah River Presbytery, specifically regarding Stamberg's views on the gift of tongues. The Presbytery's decision not to find a theological difference was annulled by the SJC.
Decision: The SJC voted 20-0 to annul the Presbytery’s decision and remanded the case for further clarification of Stamberg’s views.
Tags: theology, church doctrine, procedural, presbytery examination

Title: RE Tom Turner v. South Florida
Summary: RE Tom Turner’s complaint concerned how his church’s session handled sexual abuse allegations and the Presbytery’s judgment against the session for inadequate responses and refusal to cooperate with the investigation.
Decision: The SJC sustained parts of the complaint and remanded the case for trial. They rejected the complainant’s argument that a trial was unnecessary.

Title: TE Michael Woodham v. South Florida
Summary: TE Woodham filed a complaint regarding South Florida Presbytery’s decision to excommunicate a teaching elder without first assigning him to a particular church, as required by the BCO.
Decision: The SJC ruled that the Presbytery did not err, and the complaint was not sustained.

Title: Mrs. Kristin Hann v. Pee Dee
Summary: Mrs. Kristin Hann’s complaint was dismissed due to her lack of standing, as she was not under the jurisdiction of the Pee Dee Presbytery.
Decision: The case was dismissed as out of order.

Title: Mrs. Kappie Reynolds v. Pee Dee
Summary: The complaint was ruled out of order because it was filed after the prescribed time limit for redress of actions by a session.
Decision: The SJC dismissed the complaint as out of order.

Title: TE Ryan Biese et al. v. Tennessee Valley
Summary: This case arose from the Presbytery's approval of Redeemer Church's session minutes, which referenced a youth group Super Bowl Party. TE Biese and others argued that the minutes should have been cited for an exception of substance based on doctrinal standards. The Presbytery did not agree and approved the minutes.
Decision: The complaint was denied. The SJC found insufficient information in the record to prove the Presbytery erred.

Title: TE Knox Baird et al. v. Grace
Summary: TE Knox Baird and other session members complained against actions taken by Grace Presbytery regarding conflicts within First Presbyterian Church of Hattiesburg, Mississippi. The conflict involved the engagement of a mediator and internal disagreements among staff and church members.
Decision: The complaint was sustained, and the SJC ruled in favor of TE Baird and others.

Title: Appeal of TE Evans v. Arizona
Summary: TE John Evans appealed his divestiture from office without censure. The Presbytery had voted to divest him due to a lack of a call for an extended period, but TE Evans contended he had already affiliated with another denomination at the time of the Presbytery’s action.
Decision: The SJC ruled in favor of TE Evans, stating that he was no longer under the jurisdiction of Arizona Presbytery at the time of the action.

Title: Appeal of TE Myers v. Illiana
Summary: This case involved the elevation of two censures: suspension from office and excommunication. TE Aaron Myers was found guilty of maltreatment of his wife and fits of anger. He was first suspended but later excommunicated without additional judicial process.
Decision: The SJC reversed the excommunication, ruling that further judicial process was necessary before such an action could be taken.

Title: REs Martinez & Cook v. Pacific
Summary: This case arose from a divided vote within Valley Presbyterian Church on whether doxologies should be permitted at the close of weekly worship services. The Presbytery initially sustained the complaint against the session's decision to disallow doxologies, but REs Martinez and Cook filed further complaints regarding the follow-up actions taken by the Shepherding Committee.
Decision: The SJC partially sustained the complaint, agreeing that the Presbytery had not provided sufficient direction to the Shepherding Committee.

Title: Mr. Timothy Psiaki v. Pacific Northwest
Summary: The complaint arose from a dispute over the exclusion of under-18 communicant members from voting at a congregational meeting to elect church officers. The complainant alleged this exclusion violated church by-laws.
Decision: The SJC ruled in favor of Mr. Psiaki, stating that the exclusion of under-18 communicants from voting was unconstitutional.

Title: BCO 40-5 Matter re Metropolitan New York
Summary: This case involved the Presbytery’s failure to redress unconstitutional actions within a local congregation, including allowing a woman to expound Scripture during a worship service and holding services without preaching before the Lord’s Supper.
Decision: The SJC instructed the Presbytery to correct its actions and ensure future adherence to constitutional requirements.

Title: BCO 40-5 Matter re Northwest Georgia
Summary: The case focused on the improper installation of three associate pastors with constitutionally deficient calls. The Presbytery’s approval of these installations was challenged.
Decision: The SJC found the Presbytery in error but did not annul the installations, citing the lack of precedent for such actions.

Title: RE Tom Turner v. South Florida
Summary: This case arose from the disaffiliation of Cross Community Church from the PCA. Mr. Turner challenged the Presbytery’s handling of the disaffiliation process and the associated censure of his church.
Decision: The SJC ruled the case administratively out of order due to the lack of standing after the church disaffiliated.

Title: RE Tom Turner v. South Florida
Summary: Similar to case 2023-15, this case involved complaints about the handling of the disaffiliation of Cross Community Church.
Decision: The SJC confirmed its previous ruling, finding the complaints administratively out of order for lack of standing.
